nativeRange.habitat: Deep coral-rich reefs and reef drop-offs, typically found at depths of 15–55 m (up to 100 m)

diet.notes: In the wild this species has been documented nibbling on the tube feet of sea urchins and the tentacles of tubeworms, as well as consuming small invertebrates. In captivity it tends to accept meaty foods including mysis shrimp, marine fish flesh, crustacean flesh, and high-quality frozen carnivore preparations; frequent small feedings (at least three times daily) are generally advised.

summary: A hardy Atlantic butterflyfish from Caribbean reefs, generally considered one of the more aquarium-adaptable members of its family. Best kept as a mated pair; reef caution advised due to invertebrate-nipping tendencies.
careNotes: The Longnose Atlantic Butterflyfish is generally considered one of the hardier butterflyfish for aquarium keeping, tending to accept frozen and prepared carnivore foods more readily than many of its relatives. It is best kept singly or as a mated pair; housing with other butterflyfish species has been known to cause aggression and stress. While generally peaceful with dissimilar tankmates, it has been reported to nip at LPS corals, gorgonians, tubeworms, feather dusters, and small snails, so reef aquarium placement warrants caution. A deep-reef environment with ample live rock, caves, and open swimming space is generally advised; the species naturally inhabits deeper reef zones (15–55 m) and tends to prefer subdued or moderate lighting. Water parameters should be maintained stably within normal marine ranges. This species forms monogamous pair bonds in the wild and is sometimes sold as mated pairs; established pairs should not be separated. Temperature cross-source disagreement noted: LiveAquaria reports 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), FishBase reports 22.9–28°C, and a third source reports 76–80°F (24.4–26.7°C); the averaged range of 23–27°C is used here. Tank size cross-source disagreement noted: LiveAquaria recommends 50 gal (~190 L), BlueZoo Aquatics recommends 75 gal (~284 L); 250 L was chosen as a cautious rounded value that accounts for the species' active swimming behavior and reef-structure needs.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been rarely documented. In the wild, pairs have been observed depositing eggs on hard surfaces during the breeding season, with larvae being planktonic before settlement. Captive reproduction is generally considered impractical under normal aquarium conditions.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (confirmed max 10.0 cm), temperature range (22.9–28°C), native distribution, taxonomy (family Chaetodontidae, order Acanthuriformes), and behavior (solitary or in pairs, nibbles invertebrates).

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (75 gal / ~284 L recommended, higher than LiveAquaria's 50 gal); adult size confirmed at 4 inches; care level listed as Intermediate.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely if ever achieved. Wild pairs spawn on hard substrates; larvae are pelagic.
